title: Psalms 12
subtitle: Help, LORD, for the Faithful Are No More

heb.paragraph: "Everyone lies (shāwʾ) to their neighbour; they flatter with their lips but harbour deception in their hearts" (v.2). A society where trust has collapsed. The lips say one thing; the heart holds another. David asks God to silence the boastful tongue (v.3) and the proud who say "we will triumph with our tongues" (v.4).

refs.note: "Every friend is a deceiver" — the prophetic parallel of social breakdown.
"The tongue is a fire" — the NT development of dangerous speech.

notes.note: "Help, LORD, for no one is faithful anymore." The cry of a society where truth has collapsed. MacArthur emphasizes the doctrinal significance of this passage within the framework of systematic theology. The text establishes principles that remain normative for the church — not merely descriptive of ancient Israel but prescriptive for how God's people should understand His character and respond in obedience.

"Our lips are our own — who is lord over us?" The arrogance of autonomous speech. No authority over the tongue. MacArthur emphasizes the doctrinal significance of this passage within the framework of systematic theology. The text establishes principles that remain normative for the church — not merely descriptive of ancient Israel but prescriptive for how God's people should understand His character and respond in obedience.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: "double heart" (lēb wā-lēb) — literally two hearts. The deceiver operates with split allegiance. Alter's literary translation draws attention to the poetic artistry of the Hebrew — rhythm, parallelism, sound play, and imagery that constitute not merely decoration but meaning. The form of the poem enacts its content: how the words are arranged is inseparable from what the words say.

Calvin: "who is lord over us?" is the serpent’s question reborn. Calvin: every claim to unrestricted speech denies God’s authority over language. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: "double heart" (bə-lēb wā-lēb) literally "with a heart and a heart" — duplicity in the organ of intention. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

kidner.source: Derek Kidner, Psalms, TOTC (2 vols., 1973/1975) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kidner: "who is lord over us?" is the ultimate assertion of human autonomy — the tongue answering to no one. The psalm’s answer: God is lord over speech. Kidner's expository approach combines close attention to the Hebrew text with pastoral application. His reading identifies the psalm's enduring relevance: the same theological realities that shaped Israel's worship continue to address the human condition with clarity and power.

vangemeren.source: Willem A. VanGemeren, Psalms, EBC Rev. (2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: VanGemeren: the psalm addresses a communication crisis: when words lose integrity, society collapses. Truth is the foundation of community. VanGemeren situates the psalm within the Psalter's canonical structure, noting how its themes connect to surrounding compositions. The Psalter is not a random anthology but a curated theological argument — each psalm contributes to the whole, and its placement shapes how it should be read and prayed.

goldingay.source: John Goldingay, Psalms, BCOT (3 vols., 2006–2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Goldingay: flattery combined with deception is worse than outright hostility. The flatterer destroys trust from within. Goldingay reads the psalm within the context of Israel's worship life, attending to its liturgical setting and its function as communal prayer. The psalm speaks both for the individual and for the congregation — personal experience is given theological significance through the grammar of corporate worship.

hist.context: David cries for help in a society where faithfulness has vanished and lies dominate. Every neighbour deceives. Boastful tongues claim autonomy: "who is lord over us?" (v.4). The crisis is linguistic: words have lost their meaning.

sections.header: Verses 5–8 — “The Words of the LORD Are Flawless”

heb.paragraph: "The words (ʾamārōṭ) of the LORD are flawless, like silver purified in a crucible, refined seven times" (v.6). Against human lies, God’s words are absolutely pure. Seven times refined — complete purification. The contrast: human speech is double-hearted; God’s speech is seven-times pure.

refs.note: "Your promises have been thoroughly tested, and your servant loves them" — the same refined-word theology.
"Every word of God is flawless" — the wisdom parallel.

notes.note: "Because the poor are plundered, I will now arise, says the LORD." God speaks in the psalm — direct divine speech responding to the cry for help.
"The words of the LORD are flawless, like silver refined seven times." Perfect purity. Against the dross of human lies, God’s words have no impurity.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: the industrial metaphor — crucible, refining, silver — grounds the theology in material process. Truth is not abstract; it is tested and proven. Alter's literary translation draws attention to the poetic artistry of the Hebrew — rhythm, parallelism, sound play, and imagery that constitute not merely decoration but meaning. The form of the poem enacts its content: how the words are arranged is inseparable from what the words say.

Calvin: the seven-fold refining is God’s word tested by every challenge and found pure every time. Calvin: Scripture endures every furnace. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: "crucible of the earth" (ʺalîl lā-ʾāreṣ) is debated — may mean "on the ground" (the smelting floor) or "to the earth" (purified completely). Either way: thoroughly refined. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

kidner.source: Derek Kidner, Psalms, TOTC (2 vols., 1973/1975) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kidner: the metallurgical image: silver purified seven times has had every trace of dross removed. God’s word contains zero deception — the opposite of the double-hearted speech of v.2. Kidner's expository approach combines close attention to the Hebrew text with pastoral application. His reading identifies the psalm's enduring relevance: the same theological realities that shaped Israel's worship continue to address the human condition with clarity and power.

vangemeren.source: Willem A. VanGemeren, Psalms, EBC Rev. (2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: VanGemeren: the psalm’s structure pivots on God’s direct speech (v.5). Human deception (vv.1–4) is answered by divine declaration (v.5). Pure words replace corrupt words. VanGemeren situates the psalm within the Psalter's canonical structure, noting how its themes connect to surrounding compositions. The Psalter is not a random anthology but a curated theological argument — each psalm contributes to the whole, and its placement shapes how it should be read and prayed.

goldingay.source: John Goldingay, Psalms, BCOT (3 vols., 2006–2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Goldingay: "seven times" is completeness. The refining is not partial. God’s words are as pure as silver can possibly be. Goldingay reads the psalm within the context of Israel's worship life, attending to its liturgical setting and its function as communal prayer. The psalm speaks both for the individual and for the congregation — personal experience is given theological significance through the grammar of corporate worship.

hist.context: God speaks: "Because the poor are plundered and the needy groan, I will now arise" (v.5). When human words fail, God’s word acts. His words are flawless — seven-times-purified silver (v.6). David trusts God’s protection even as the wicked strut freely (vv.7–8).

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIV“Because the poor are plundered and the needy groan, I will now arise,” says the Lord. “I will protect them from those who malign them.”ESV\u201cBecause the poor are plundered, because the needy groan, I will now arise,\u201d says the LORD; \u201cI will place him in the safety for which he longs.\u201dNIVwho freely strut about when what is vile is honored by the human race.ESVOn every side the wicked prowl, as vileness is exalted among the children of man.

rec.text: ""Help, LORD, for no one is faithful anymore." The cry of a society where truth has collapsed."

rows.label: The Lie — 12:1–4
rows.text: "No one is faithful." Double hearts, boastful tongues. "Who is lord over us?"

hebtext.note: "Everyone lies (shāwʾ) to their neighbour; they flatter with their lips but harbour deception in their hearts" (v.2). A society where trust has collapsed. The lips say one thing; the heart holds another. David asks God to silence the boastful tongue (v.3) and the proud who say "we will triumph with our tongues" (v.4).

"The words (ʾamārōṭ) of the LORD are flawless, like silver purified in a crucible, refined seven times" (v.6). Against human lies, God’s words are absolutely pure. Seven times refined — complete purification. The contrast: human speech is double-hearted; God’s speech is seven-times pure.

themes.note: Holiness peaks: God’s words are seven-times refined. Justice: the poor are plundered; God arises. Sovereignty: God speaks and acts.
